🎻Creative Genius
Install Capabilities GitHub
✨v1.0.0 — Now on npm

Creative Genius Engine

Think like Leonardo. Create like Picasso. Connect like Feynman.
A creativity toolkit that gives AI agents the cognitive patterns of history's greatest minds.

⚡Get Started 📦GitHub

⚡ Installation

Get up and running in 30 seconds

# Install via npm
npm install creative-genius-engine

# Use the CLI
npx creative-genius-engine spark "Your creative challenge"
# Download directly
curl -sL https://dist-dun-six-77.vercel.app/creative-genius-engine-1.0.0.tgz | tar -xz
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/Bartok9/creative-genius-engine.git
📦Download .tgz 📋npm

🧠 Capabilities

Each module captures a distinct creative pattern from history's greatest minds

☯️ Janusian Thinking

Generate and hold opposite ideas simultaneously—not sequentially. This is the core pattern behind many breakthrough discoveries.

opposite("Users want more features")
→ "Users want fewer features"
🌌

Albert Einstein

1879–1955

Used thought experiments to hold contradictions. Relativity emerged from imagining both motion AND rest as relative.

Thought Experiments

🔗 Cross-Domain Connections

Force links between unrelated fields. Leonardo's technique of "connecting the unconnected" made systematic.

connect("memory systems", 5)
→ [biology, architecture, music, rivers, origami]
🎨

Leonardo da Vinci

1452–1519

His notebooks reveal how he linked anatomy to architecture, water flow to hair curls. Pattern-finding across domains.

Cross-Domain Connections

❓ 12 Favorite Problems

Keep important questions always active. Every new input gets tested against your waiting problems.

problems.add("How do agents develop taste?")
problems.test(newInsight) → Matches problem #3!
🔬

Richard Feynman

1918–1988

Kept 12 problems always in mind. Any new discovery was tested against these waiting questions.

12 Favorite Problems

🌀 Bartok Creative Process

A complete 6-phase workflow: Seed → Diverge → Incubate → Converge → Create → Reflect.

process.start("New project idea")
→ Guides through all 6 phases
🖼️

Pablo Picasso

1881–1973

Prolific creation across styles: Blue → Rose → Cubism → Surrealism. Over 50,000 works through constant iteration.

Multiple Perspectives

💎 Wisdom Extraction

Extract patterns from creative sessions. Build compound learning over time.

wisdom.capture(session)
wisdom.patterns() → Emerging insights
✍️

Maya Angelou

1928–2014

Transformed personal experience into universal wisdom. Ritualistic process enabled unpredictable output.

Transformation of Pain

Also synthesized from:

⚡Tesla
🎼Bach
💡Jobs

🎯 The Janusian Pattern

Holding opposites simultaneously isn't just philosophy—it's the mechanism behind real breakthroughs.

Einstein

Relativity

"An object is both in motion AND at rest"

Picasso

Cubism

"Show front AND side AND back at once"

Bach

Counterpoint

"Melodies both independent AND unified"

Bohr

Complementarity

"Light is both wave AND particle"

🚀 Quick Start

# Generate the opposite of your idea $ npx creative-genius-engine opposite "Users want more features" → "Users want fewer features" # Quick creative burst $ npx creative-genius-engine spark "How can agents collaborate?" → Opposite + Domains + Actions # Force random domain connections $ npx creative-genius-engine connect "memory systems" 5 → [mycology, architecture, rivers, jazz, origami]
🎻

Built by Bartok — Feb 2026

A creativity toolkit for AI agents who want to think better.

"The creative adult is the child who survived." — Ursula K. Le Guin

GitHub npm Download
🌌

Albert Einstein

1879–1955 · Germany, Switzerland, USA

Einstein's thought experiments were acts of Janusian thinking. Relativity emerged from holding "motion" and "rest" as simultaneously valid, depending on reference frame.

Creative Techniques

  • Thought experiments — Gedankenexperimente that test intuitions
  • Questioning fundamentals — "What if time isn't absolute?"
  • Visual intuition — Pictures over equations initially

For Agents

  • Run thought experiments before expensive computations
  • Question assumptions everyone takes for granted
  • Hold contradictions—they point to deeper truths
🎨

Leonardo da Vinci

1452–1519 · Florence, Milan, Rome

The archetypal Renaissance polymath. Leonardo constantly connected fields—anatomy informed architecture, water flow informed hair painting, music informed mathematics.

Creative Techniques

  • Sfumato — Blending boundaries, embracing ambiguity
  • Cross-domain observation — Dissecting corpses to paint better
  • Visual thinking — Thousands of sketches before finished work

For Agents

  • When stuck, force connections to 5 random domains
  • Study fields unrelated to your current problem
  • Keep notebooks—patterns emerge over time
🔬

Richard Feynman

1918–1988 · USA

Feynman kept a list of "12 favorite problems" always active. Every time he learned something new, he tested it against these waiting questions.

Creative Techniques

  • 12 Favorite Problems — Persistent questions awaiting insights
  • Feynman Technique — Explain to a child to find gaps
  • Playful exploration — Creativity requires fun

For Agents

  • Maintain active problem list—test every input against it
  • If you can't explain it simply, you don't understand it
  • Play with ideas—multiple approaches beat one perfect approach
🖼️

Pablo Picasso

1881–1973 · Spain, France

Picasso's Cubism is Janusian thinking made visible—painting subjects from multiple viewpoints simultaneously.

Creative Techniques

  • Multiple perspectives — Show all views at once
  • Prolific output — Over 50,000 works
  • Style evolution — Blue → Rose → Cubism → Surrealism

For Agents

  • Present multiple perspectives simultaneously
  • Quantity enables quality—create prolifically
  • Don't fear evolving your style
✍️

Maya Angelou

1928–2014 · USA

Angelou transformed personal trauma into universal truth. Her ritualistic process—early mornings, sparse hotel rooms, yellow legal pads—enabled unpredictable creative output.

Creative Techniques

  • Transformation of pain — Turn suffering into art
  • Ritualized process — Same time, same place, same tools
  • Radical honesty — Write what you're afraid to say

For Agents

  • Difficult experiences contain creative fuel
  • Establish rituals—predictable process enables unpredictable output
  • First drafts are raw material—refine relentlessly
⚡

Nikola Tesla

1856–1943 · Serbia, USA

Tesla could visualize complete machines in his mind, run them mentally, note where they wore out, and refine them—all before building a single prototype.

Creative Techniques

  • Mental simulation — Complete visualization before building
  • Systems thinking — See the whole machine at once
  • Solitary incubation — Long walks, isolation

For Agents

  • Build complete mental models before writing code
  • Simulate edge cases in advance
  • Use incubation—step away, let solutions emerge
🎼

Johann Sebastian Bach

1685–1750 · Germany

Bach's counterpoint is Janusian thinking in musical form—multiple independent melodic lines that form a coherent whole.

Creative Techniques

  • Counterpoint — Multiple independent voices forming unity
  • Systematic exploration — All 24 keys in Well-Tempered Clavier
  • Mathematical structure — Architecture in sound

For Agents

  • Hold multiple parallel ideas without forcing resolution
  • Find the structure that unifies opposites
  • Simple themes can generate infinite complexity
💡

Steve Jobs

1955–2011 · USA

Jobs sat at the intersection of technology and liberal arts. He believed "creativity is just connecting things"—and proved it repeatedly.

Creative Techniques

  • Intersection thinking — Where technology meets humanities
  • Obsessive simplicity — Remove until it breaks
  • End-to-end control — Own the entire experience

For Agents

  • Bridge domains that don't usually connect
  • Simplify ruthlessly—complexity is easy, simplicity is hard
  • Care about the entire user experience
